prompt:
I want a referee-grade tic-tac-toe for our zsh-only boxes (zsh 5.9) — no bot, no cleverness, just a program that takes a stream of moves and enforces the rules to the letter so two people can pipe a game at it and trust the verdict. Write `ttt.zsh` in the repo root, zsh-native (1-indexed thinking, no word-splitting surprises) and pure zsh: the harness runs it with an EMPTY PATH, so nothing external can be forked. It takes no arguments — anything on the command line prints `usage: ttt.zsh` to stderr and exits 2. Moves come one per line on stdin: a row letter a-c plus a column digit 1-3, like `b2`. Case doesn't matter on input (`B2` is `b2`), but everything you print is lowercase. Blank lines are skipped silently. X moves first and turns alternate. For each legal move print `

plays ` and then the board: a header line of two spaces then `1 2 3`, and one line per row — the row letter, then the three cells as `X`, `O` or `.`, single spaces between everything. A move onto an occupied square prints `square is taken`; garbage prints `invalid move: ` exactly as it arrived — neither costs the mover their turn. After every move, judge the game: three in a row/column/diagonal for the mover prints `

wins` right after the board, exit 0 (a ninth-move win is a win, not a draw); a full board otherwise prints `draw`, exit 0; either way stop reading input. If stdin ends while the game is still live, print `game aborted` and exit 1. `zsh test_ttt.zsh` pins whole game transcripts — every render byte-exact — for wins in all directions, a draw, a ninth-move win, mixed-case input, and the retry rules. Don't touch the test file.
